text: Hello everyone, this is Roxanne Liu from Oakland, California. This video is going to demonstrate the steps for an efficient right angle hernia flap closure. We're first going to focus on the left hand. So you can see this is the right side. And when your left hand is about to grab the needle, make sure the thumb is up. This allows your left wrist to pronate and pull the needle out with the curvature of the needle so that you don't rip the perineum. And frequently, for an inguinal flap, the peritoneum is very thin, so you don't want to put any stress or tension on the peritoneal flap. So again, thumb is going to be up.

The surgeon demonstrates the technique for handling the needle during a right angle hernia flap closure. The left hand pronates to pull the needle out with its curvature, while the right hand supinates to efficiently take the next bite. The suture is managed by pulling it to the left lower quadrant.

The surgeon pulls the needle out of the screen, demonstrating spatial awareness. The left hand pulls the suture while the right hand pins it between the needle and needle driver, pushing the suture taut. The closure progresses smoothly with four to six throws, as the right hand pulls the suture to the left lower quadrant and the left hand aligns it with the suture line.

So the general principles of needle and suture handling are use the curve of the needle, avoid dropping the needle, use both instruments to advance the needle. Always pull or push the suture in the direction of the flap closure so that you do not rip the peritoneum. For the left hand, always have your thumb up and pronate the left wrist. And for the right hand, have the thumb pointed towards the chest or down and supinate the wrist. So here's just a completion of a closure. Just watch the choreography of the two hands. I never drop the needle. There's no wasted movements. As soon as I pull the needle out, the left hand presents the needle to the right hand and allows the right hand to grab the needle in the perfect position for suturing. I don't have to manipulate the needle much. I don't drop the needle. This allows you to close the flap very efficiently.

The surgeon uses a suction cannula to evacuate the pocket, leaving the last few sutures intentionally loose. The bedside assistant removes the needle driver and introduces the suction cannula, which is open to air, allowing intra-abdominal pressure to expel CO₂ from the pocket. The surgeon runs the suture back two throws before the assistant reintroduces the needle driver. The suture is cut flush against the peritoneum without pulling it taut to prevent the tail from springing back.
